name: Crossplane
what_it_does: Kubernetes-native infrastructure management framework that extends the Kubernetes API with Custom Resource Definitions (CRDs) to provision and manage cloud infrastructure (AWS, GCP, Azure, etc.) using kubectl and standard Kubernetes tooling. No separate REST API — everything is managed through the Kubernetes API server.
use_cases: Managing cloud infrastructure via kubectl and GitOps workflows (Flux, ArgoCD)
Building platform engineering abstractions — Composite Resource Definitions (XRDs) as internal developer APIs
Multi-cloud infrastructure provisioning with consistent Kubernetes-native interface
Self-service infrastructure for developers within a Kubernetes cluster
Policy-driven infrastructure governance via Kubernetes admission controllers
not_for: Teams without an existing Kubernetes cluster — Crossplane requires Kubernetes to run
Simple infrastructure provisioning without platform engineering goals
Non-Kubernetes-native workflows where Terraform or Pulumi fit better
Organizations without Kubernetes expertise on the platform team
best_when: Your platform team wants to expose infrastructure provisioning as Kubernetes CRDs so developers can request infrastructure with kubectl, and you are already running Kubernetes for application workloads.
avoid_when: You don't have Kubernetes or want to avoid the operational overhead of running a control plane cluster. Terraform or Pulumi are simpler for teams without Kubernetes investment.

auth.notes: Authentication and authorization are handled entirely through Kubernetes RBAC. Agents interact via kubeconfig or in-cluster service account tokens. Fine-grained RBAC on CRDs is supported. Cloud provider credentials are stored as Kubernetes Secrets and referenced by ProviderConfig resources.

pricing.free_tier_limits: Fully free and open source (Apache 2.0). No usage limits.
pricing.paid_tiers: Upbound (commercial Crossplane company) offers Upbound Cloud and managed control planes — pricing varies. Enterprise support available.
pricing.notes: Crossplane itself is free. You pay for the underlying cloud resources it provisions and optionally for Upbound managed control planes if you use them instead of self-hosting.

performance.rate_limits: Bound by Kubernetes API server rate limits (typically 200-400 req/s) and underlying cloud provider limits. Crossplane uses Kubernetes watch/reconcile loop — not request/response.

agent_readiness.error_message_notes: Crossplane exposes status conditions on CRDs following the Kubernetes Ready/Synced/Healthy pattern. Error details are in resource status.conditions[].message fields. Errors from underlying providers (AWS, GCP) are surfaced but can be verbose and provider-specific. Requires parsing Kubernetes events and conditions rather than simple error codes.
agent_readiness.idempotency_notes: Kubernetes declarative model is inherently idempotent. Applying the same manifest twice is a no-op if state matches. Crossplane reconciliation loop continuously drives actual state toward desired state.
known_agent_gotchas: No direct REST API — agents must use kubectl, Kubernetes client libraries (client-go, python-kubernetes), or a Kubernetes API server proxy
Infrastructure provisioning is asynchronous via reconciliation loop — agents must watch resource Ready/Synced conditions, not expect immediate completion
Requires a running Kubernetes cluster as a control plane — significant operational overhead vs Terraform/Pulumi
Composition logic (XRDs/Compositions) requires Crossplane expertise to author — complex YAML patching syntax
Deleting a Crossplane managed resource deletes the underlying cloud resource — agents must handle deletion carefully
Provider credentials must be stored as Kubernetes Secrets in the control plane cluster — secret rotation is a manual process
CRD schema versioning can cause breaking changes on provider upgrades — test provider upgrades in staging

security_score_components.security_notes: RBAC on CRDs provides fine-grained access control via Kubernetes ClusterRoles. Cloud provider credentials are stored as Kubernetes Secrets — no external secrets management built in (recommend External Secrets Operator). TLS everywhere via Kubernetes. No network exposure beyond the Kubernetes API server required.
